summary:
- Casio's legacy spans from the 1974 Casiotron to the 2023 NFT metaverse pivot, but the core story is one of relentless innovation and adaptation.
- The F-91W's unintended role as an IED timer and the 1983 BP-100's blood-pressure sensor illustrate how Casio's products have become part of the texture of modern life and even global security and health.
- From the 1980s calculator watches to the 2020s smartwatch collaborations, Casio has repeatedly shown a willingness to cannibalize its own products to stay relevant, even if it means abandoning the very features that once defined the brand.
- The community's nostalgia for the 1980s-90s calculator and databank watches is palpable, but the company has largely moved on to G-Shock and smartwatch partnerships, leaving some to wonder if the original spirit of utility and durability is being lost in the pursuit of fashion and tech collaborations.
- Despite the company's storied history of innovation, Casio's current strategy appears to be to focus on the G-Shock line and smartwatch partnerships, which may be leaving some of the most passionate users behind, as they still want the classic utility and durability that made the brand iconic.
